name: Huli Jing
description: The Huli Jing, literally 'fox spirit' or 'fox demon', is a mythical creature originating from Chinese folklore. These spirits are believed to be foxes who have attained supernatural abilities through longevity and dedicated cultivation. They are known for their cunning, shapeshifting abilities, and capacity for both benevolent and malevolent actions. Huli Jing are often depicted as beautiful women who seduce men, drain their life force, or lead them astray. However, some Huli Jing are benevolent, offering guidance, protection, or even companionship to humans. The level of power and influence a Huli Jing possesses is often tied to their age and the number of tails they have, with nine-tailed foxes (Jiǔwěihú) being the most powerful and ancient. Their motivations are complex, ranging from a desire for immortality to simple mischief. Stories of Huli Jing are prevalent in Chinese literature, opera, and folklore, highlighting their enduring presence in Chinese culture and imagination. They represent a blend of beauty, danger, and supernatural power, captivating audiences for centuries. The transformations they undergo are symbolic of personal growth and the potential for change, whether for good or evil. Their tales serve as moral lessons and cautionary tales, exploring themes of temptation, deception, and the consequences of human desires.
subtitle: Cunning shapeshifting fox spirits
appearance: Huli Jing typically appear as foxes, often with an increasing number of tails as they age and gain power. The most powerful Huli Jing, the Jiǔwěihú, possess nine tails. They can also shapeshift into beautiful human forms, most commonly appearing as alluring women. Their human forms are often described as possessing captivating beauty, with sharp features and piercing eyes. They may also retain subtle fox-like characteristics, such as pointed ears or a mischievous glint in their eyes.
habitat: Huli Jing are often found in forests, mountains, and secluded areas away from human settlements. They may also inhabit abandoned temples or ancient tombs. Some Huli Jing choose to live amongst humans, blending in with society using their shapeshifting abilities.
behavior: Huli Jing exhibit a wide range of behaviors, from mischievous trickery to outright malevolence. Some are benevolent, offering guidance and protection to humans. Others are predatory, seducing men to drain their life force or leading them to ruin. Their behavior is often driven by their desire for immortality, power, or simply a love of mischief. They are known for their cunning and intelligence, able to manipulate situations to their advantage.

keyMyths.title: Daji, the Nine-Tailed Fox Spirit
keyMyths.description: Daji was a nine-tailed fox spirit who possessed a beautiful woman and became the consort of the last Shang dynasty emperor, Zhou. She used her influence to corrupt the emperor and bring about the dynasty's downfall, showcasing the Huli Jing's destructive potential.

keyMyths.title: The Fox Spirit and the Scholar
keyMyths.description: Many stories depict Huli Jing seducing scholars, sometimes with malicious intent and other times forming genuine bonds. These tales explore themes of temptation, morality, and the blurring lines between the human and supernatural realms.

sources.author: Kang-i Sun Chang
sources.work: The Cambridge History of Chinese Literature
sources.description: Provides historical context and analysis of fox spirit narratives in Chinese literature.
sources.text: Provides historical context and analysis of fox spirit narratives in Chinese literature.

sources.author: Wilt L. Idema
